query: dental plaque composition and adhesion mechanisms

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Dental plaque is a complex biofilm, which is the colonization of bacteria enclosed by a protective matrix. This matrix is composed of extracellular polysaccharide and glycoproteins providing a protective environment for microbes in the dental biofilm. [18] This component of the dental biofilm makes it 1000 to 1500 times more resistant to antimicrobial agents. [2] The various circulatory channels present in the biofilm aid in the distribution of many nutrients and excretion of the generated metabolic wastes. "Quorum sensing" is a mode of communication between the microcolonies of bacteria within the dental biofilm. [19] "Autoinducers" are molecules secreted by microbes; the concentration of these molecules helps in regulating bacterial gene expression. [19] The biofilm, by different pH concentrations and metabolites, has various microenvironments within the biofilm. These microenvironments make the ecosystem suitable for a variety of microbes inhabiting in the same dental plaque. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Periodontium -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Initially, the gingival crevicular fluid amount is increased due to vascular changes in response to the initial insult. At this benign stage, polymorphonuclear neutrophils are attracted to the lesion site, and T lymphocytes are responsible for fibroblasts. The early lesion is characterized by redness. At this stage, PMNs clear and breakdown the collagen fibers, which increases the previously made space for infiltrates. At this stage, the established lesion is dominated by B cells and leukocyte aggregation. This initiates the lesion side transformation by changing the junctional and sulcular epithelium into an extremely vulnerable epithelium called the pocket epithelium. This is apparent as bleeding upon gentle gingival manipulation. An advanced lesion characterized as loss of gingival fibers and alveolar bone is caused by the migration of biofilm into the pocket and creating an environment for anaerobic bacterias' proliferation. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Fluoride Release
retrieved_docs.content: The bulk of the GIC matrix plays a vital role in the fluoride release capacity. The fluoride release is influenced by intrinsic factors such as the composition of the matrix, setting mechanism, and fluoride content, and extrinsic factors like the pH of saliva, plaque, pellicle formation, liquid-to-powder ratio, mixing, setting and curing time, and the amount of exposed surface. Bleaching and tooth brushing do not significantly affect fluoride release. [13] The coverage of the surface of GIC with an adhesive or surface protective agent results in a 1.4 to 4-fold decrease in fluoride release.

retrieved_docs.title: Gingivitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The early lesion is consistent with delayed hypersensitivity. It usually appears after 1 week from the beginning of plaque deposition. In this stage, the clinical signs of gingivitis, such as redness and bleeding from the gingiva, start appearing. The inflammatory cells that predominate in this lesion are lymphocytes, accounting for 75% of the total, and macrophages. A small number of plasma cells are also seen. Along with the inflammatory infiltration that occupies 5% to 15% of the connective tissue of the gingival margin, there is a loss of collagen in the affected area that reaches 60% to 70%. Furthermore, the local fibroblasts undergo a series of pathological changes, and the gingival fluid flow and the number of leukocytes migrating to the region continue to increase. Neutrophils and mononuclear cells are also increased in the junctional epithelium. The duration of the early lesion has not yet been determined; it can remain for longer than previously expected. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The initial layer deposited on the surface of teeth in the formation of dental plaque is "acquired pellicle." [20] This layer is formed within seconds of the exposure of tooth surfaces, followed by the initial attachment of the early colonizers of the biofilm. Streptococcus and Actinomyces species are the primary colonizers which are gram-positive, facultative bacteria. [20] The "adhesin receptors" present on the surface of primary colonizers bind with the proline-rich proteins of the pellicle. This binding leads to revealing of receptor sites known as "cryptitope" which further leads to coaggregation. [21] Gradually there is a layer by layer deposition of dental plaque leading to a deficiency of oxygen in the environment eventually leading to colonization of anaerobic bacteria. [22] The bridging microbe between primary and secondary colonizers is Fusobacterium species. [22] The gradual shift of these aerobic conditions to anaerobic condition marks the progression of gingivitis to periodontitis. Socransky et al. in a study divided the microbes into microbial complexes based on colors. [23] [5] Red and orange complexes bacteria present in the subgingival area are intricately associated with periodontal disease. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The now infected and necrotic root canal system is a habitat that allows for the growth and establishment of a mixed, predominately anaerobic flora, which mainly grows in sessile biofilms and aggregates or co-aggregates. [6] The microbial invaders or their by-products can advance into the periapex of the root. In response, the host mounts a defensive mechanism through various cells, intracellular mediators, and humoral antibodies. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: To understand the pathophysiology of periodontal disease, it is essential to know about the complex dental biofilm as well as the Immune response associated with the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Gingivitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: This is the most common cause of gingivitis. Plaque is a thin film that forms on the tooth surface due to poor oral hygiene. If not regularly removed, it can harden up and form calculus. As plaque harbors a large number of bacteria, inflammation can occur in the gingival tissue. Some local factors can contribute to the formation of plaque, such as crowding of teeth, which makes plaque removal difficult. As misaligned teeth often require orthodontic correction, cleaning difficulty increases, resulting in the accumulation of more plaque. Furthermore, a dental prosthesis that does not have an adequate fit or is not properly finished can also act as a nidus for plaque accumulation. In children, tooth eruption is frequently associated with gingivitis as plaque accumulation tends to increase in the area where primary teeth are exfoliating, and permanent teeth are erupting, as oral hygiene may be difficult to maintain in these areas. This is referred to as eruptive gingivitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Periodontium -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Peri-implantitis is due to the failure to create the junction between the implant and the oral mucosa, leaving the underlying connective tissue exposed to the permanent oral microbiome and creating chronic inflammation of gingival and bone tissue. [11] Knowledge of histology helped to demonstrate that coating the peri-implant pocket with hyaluronic acid gel reduces inflammation in peri-implantitis by improving the connection between the core and recipients’ connective tissue through reducing inflammation. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Function -- Pathophysiology of Dental Caries
retrieved_docs.content: When repeated events of mineral dissolution happen, and the number of subsurface minerals lost surpasses the ones gained over a long period, the disease will show its first clinical sign : a white spot. [8] The progression of the white spot and the next step of the disease, a cavitated caries, can be prevented, reversed, or arrested by controlling the caries process. Still, the pH fluctuations in the biofilm are due to bacterial metabolism; therefore, loss and redeposition of minerals are inevitable. That is why we have to think about the initial phases of caries as a natural process that cannot be avoided. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Function -- Pathophysiology of Dental Caries
retrieved_docs.content: All the tooth surfaces are susceptible to caries throughout an individual's lifetime. But, the disease will not occur unless teeth are exposed to cariogenic biofilm and frequent sugar consumption. The modern concept of caries also considers other factors, such as sociodemographic situation, access to dental care, and fluoride exposure, which are significant but not decisive to develop the disease. For all of this, dental caries should be described as a complex biofilm-mediated affection linked mostly to frequent ingestion of sugars and poor oral hygiene combined with inadequate fluoride exposure. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Mechanism of Bonding
retrieved_docs.content: GIC chemically (ionic) bonds to the tooth structure (enamel and dentin). GIC bonds better to enamel than dentin because of the higher inorganic content in enamel. The mechanism of GIC adhesion to the tooth's inorganic structure involves a chelation reaction between the carboxyl groups of the polyacrylic acid and the calcium in the hydroxyapatite crystals of the tooth.

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Issues of Concern -- Fluoride Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride used at high concentrations, more than 2500 ppm, can penetrate the dental biofilm, deliver fluoride to the tooth surface, and concentrate in incipient lesions. Thus, fluoride decreases enamel demineralization and increases remineralization. A higher fluoride concentration also prolongs the fluoride retention in the oral cavity by forming a fluoride reservoir (calcium fluoride-like deposits) on the tooth surface and in dental biofilms. It is worth noting that very high fluoride levels have a transitory bactericidal effect. However, they usually require frequent professional applications, which is impractical for both patients and professionals. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Gingivitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: This stage is characterized by an acute exudative inflammatory response, a raised gingival fluid flow, and the migration of neutrophils from the blood vessel of the subgingival plexus located in the gingival connective tissue to the gingival sulcus. An alteration of the matrix of the connective tissue located next to vessels results in the accumulation of fibrin in the area. The initial lesion is seen within 4 days of the initiation of plaque accumulation. There is a destruction of collagen caused by collagenase and other enzymes secreted by the neutrophils. About 5% to 10% of the connective tissue is occupied by the inflammatory infiltrate in this stage. [12] 2. Early lesion

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Issues of Concern -- Fluoride Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Fluor (F) must be present in the right place and at the right time to interfere with demineralization and remineralization events. However, even low ppm values are sufficient to fulfill its effect. When pH drops up to 4.5, fluorapatite (FA) is formed while hydroxyapatite (HA) is dissolved. If F is present, the enamel dissolution decreases because the enamel recovers those lost minerals as fluorapatite. Furthermore, the action of F is complemented by its natural effect on remineralization, enhancing the redeposition of calcium and phosphate present in the biofilm fluid when the pH rises. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 177
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic group Dis‑orders of choroid and retina (H30‑H36) comprises a wide spectrum of choroidal and retinal pathologies that share mechanisms such as vascular compromise, inflammation, neovascular growth, degeneration, trauma, postoperative change, and systemic disease influence. Structural and degenerative entities include partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, hereditary choroidal dystrophy (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي), calcific degeneration (تنكس حصوي), choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), senile choroidal atrophy (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية), age‑related choroidal atrophy, secondary choroidal atrophy spread (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), peripapillary/choroidal sclerosis (partial حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي and total حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي), and serous, ocular, or hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين, انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Traumatic and postoperative sequelae are represented by choroidal rupture (التمزق المشيمي), choroidal

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1160
retrieved_docs.content: hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي) reflect abnormal calcium accumulation within the choroidal stroma. Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroidea is a common locus for diverse pathological processes, and recognizing the specific type of choroidal involvement—whether hemorrhagic, inflammatory, traumatic, dystrophic, detachment‑related, or depositional—is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate management, and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1178
retrieved_docs.content: postoperative choroïdoretinal scar (litteken; choroïdoretinaal, postoperatief), posttraumatic choroid scar, posttraumatic chorioretinal scar, and generic choroidal scar (litteken; choroidea). All of these entities illustrate that disorders of the choroid and retina involve inflammatory, infectious, vascular, degenerative, traumatic, sclerotic, calcific, detachment, and scar‑forming mechanisms, and that precise recognition of both English and Arabic terminology is essential for accurate classification, diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic planning in ophthalmic patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634

Finally, choroidal scarring (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) denotes fibrotic remodeling after injury or inflammation. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in ocular disease, highlighting the need for careful clinical assessment and targeted management of choroidal involvement across diverse pathological contexts.

Perivascular deposits (بُقَعُ روث) are also recorded. Collectively, these entities illustrate the intersection of systemic vascular disease with specific retinal and choroidal hemorrhagic findings, neovascular disorders, inflammatory and infectious processes, traumatic and postoperative sequelae, and degenerative changes, providing a comprehensive framework for diagnosis, therapeutic decision‑making, and prognostic assessment in ophthalmic care.

Calcific processes are noted as calcific degeneration (تنكس حصوي), partial choroidal calcification around the macula (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), and total choroidal calcification around the macula (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي). Hereditary and structural abnormalities include genetic choroidal hemorrhage (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي), retinal‑choroidal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية), choroidal rupture, partial peripapillary choroidal sclerosis, total peripapillary choroidal sclerosis, hereditary choroidal sclerosis, and chorioretinal scar. Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroidea is a common site of hemorrhage, scar formation, dystrophy, inflammation, infection, trauma, postoperative change, and metastatic involvement. Degenerative and atrophic lesions comprise partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, senile choroidal atrophy, secondary choroidal atrophy (including spread), calcific degeneration, choroidal degeneration (“تنكس حصوي”, “تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة”), hereditary/choroidal dystrophy (familial choroidal dystrophy, hereditary choroidal atrophy “حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي”), and secondary choroidal atrophy related to aging. Traumatic and post‑injury sequelae form another subgroup, with entities such as Posttraumatic chorioretinal scar, التمزق المشيمي (choroidal tear), and littken; choroidea (choroidal scar). Scarring and fibrotic changes are further represented by Disseminated scar of retina AND choroid, ندبة مشيمية شبكية (chorioretinal scar), and other choroidal scar lesions. Degenerative and calcific processes are also included: تنكس حصوي, تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal degeneration), حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي (global choroidal calcification), حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي (hereditary choroidal calcification), and انفصال المشيمية المصلية (serous choroidal detachment) as well as انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي (hemorrhagic choroidal detachment). Partial and complete choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي; حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) and hereditary choroidal sclerosis (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي) reflect fibrotic stiffening. Focal choroidal degeneration (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة) and chorioretinal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) illustrate localized tissue loss and scarring that may involve both choroid and retina. The choroidea serves as the anatomic substrate for additional inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, postoperative and metastatic processes, including the entities listed above, metastatic disseminated retinitis and retinochoroiditis, partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, familial retinal dystrophy of the choroid, and choroidal tears. Massive choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي, نزيف مشيمي باثق) exemplifies the choroidea’s role as a site for hemorrhagic events. Collectively, these interconnected entities depict a comprehensive view of posterior‑segment ocular

Language‑specific eponymous entities also include familial choroidal membrane disorder (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية) and choroidal rupture (التمزق المشيمي). Collectively, these interrelated entities provide a comprehensive framework for diagnosing, classifying, and managing ocular, vascular, and systemic diseases that affect the choroid, retina, and related structures, emphasizing the choroidea as the common locus for inflammatory, degenerative, hemorrhagic, depositional, traumatic, postoperative, metastatic, and scar‑forming processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1082
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is the principal anatomic locus for a broad spectrum of ocular disorders, including hemorrhagic, inflammatory, infectious, degenerative, postoperative, and metastatic conditions. Hemorrhagic involvement is exemplified by choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي). Inflammatory diseases affecting the choroid encompass disseminated choroiditis, disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, focal choroiditis, juxtapapillary focal choroiditis and chorioretinitis, familial choroidal lesions (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), choroid‑retinal inflammation (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية), and focal, unspecified choroid‑retinal inflammation (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد). Degenerative processes include partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, stone‑like choroidal atrophy (تنكس حصوي), general choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), and peri‑optic choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي). Post‑inflammatory sequelae
